_purpose: INTERNAL FILE - not linked from any page, not in sitemap.xml, disallowed in robots.txt. Not meant for site visitors. Before writing new articles for this site, read this file and make sure no new topic duplicates or substantially overlaps an entry below. Each entry's 'covers' field summarizes the specific angle/scope of that article so overlap is easy to spot.

articles.title: What's New for 2026: How the One Big Beautiful Bill Act Affects Your Taxes
articles.covers: Every filing season brings small adjustments — updated brackets, a slightly higher standard deduction.

articles.title: Standard vs. Itemized Deductions in 2026: Which Saves You More?
articles.covers: Every filer makes this choice, whether they realize it or not: take the flat standard deduction, or add up specific deductible expenses and itemize instead.

articles.title: Marginal vs. Effective Tax Rate: Understanding What You Really Pay
articles.covers: US federal income tax is progressive: your income is sliced into layers (brackets), and each layer is taxed at its own rate.

articles.title: The Complete Guide to Self-Employment Tax: Understanding the 15.3%
articles.covers: If you freelance, contract, consult, or run a side business, you face a tax that W-2 employees never see written on a pay stub: the Self-Employment Tax (SE tax).

articles.title: Child Tax Credit 2026: Rules, Amounts, and Phase-Outs Explained
articles.covers: The Child Tax Credit (CTC) is one of the most valuable tax benefits available to families, often worth more than any single deduction.

articles.title: Long-Term Capital Gains Tax: The 0%, 15%, and 20% Brackets Explained
articles.covers: Sell an appreciated asset — stocks, mutual funds, real estate, a stake in a business — and capital gains tax comes into play.

articles.title: Tax Filing Deadlines 2026: Every Date You Need to Know
articles.covers: Missing a tax deadline costs money even in years you would have owed nothing — a late-filing penalty can apply on top of a late-payment penalty, and both accrue interest.

articles.title: Tax Withholding and Form W-4: How to Stop Overpaying or Owing at Tax Time
articles.covers: A massive refund every April can feel like a bonus, but it's really an interest-free loan you gave the government all year.

articles.title: Commonly Overlooked Tax Deductions and Credits: A Checklist
articles.covers: Taking the standard deduction doesn't lock you out of everything else.

articles.title: 401(k), Traditional IRA, and Roth IRA: How Each One Is Taxed
articles.covers: A 401(k), a traditional IRA, and a Roth IRA all exist to help you save for retirement, but they're taxed in almost opposite ways.

articles.title: Married Filing Jointly vs. Separately: Which Should You Choose?
articles.covers: Married couples get to choose between two filing statuses every year, and the choice isn't locked in — you can pick whichever is better each time you file.

articles.title: Head of Household Filing Status: Who Qualifies and Why It Pays to Check
articles.covers: Of the five filing statuses, Head of Household (HoH) is the one people most often qualify for without realizing it — and the one people incorrectly claim without realizing they don't.

articles.title: Who Counts as a Dependent? Qualifying Child vs. Qualifying Relative, Explained
articles.covers: The Child Tax Credit, Head of Household filing status, the Dependent Care Credit, and several other benefits all start with the same question: does this person legally count as your dependent?

articles.title: How Bonuses, Stock Options, and RSUs Are Actually Taxed
articles.covers: Equity compensation and bonuses are taxed as income like anything else, but the timing and mechanics differ enough from a regular paycheck that they consistently surprise people — usually either at withholding time or th

articles.title: The Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT): What It Is and Who It Actually Affects
articles.covers: The Alternative Minimum Tax has a reputation for being mysterious and vaguely threatening, mostly because very few filers ever interact with it directly.

articles.title: Education Tax Credits: American Opportunity Credit vs. Lifetime Learning Credit
articles.covers: Two federal credits can offset the cost of tuition and related expenses, and they're easy to mix up because they cover overlapping situations.

articles.title: HSA vs. FSA: The Real Tax Differences
articles.covers: Health Savings Accounts and Flexible Spending Accounts both let you set aside pre-tax money for medical costs, and the two get confused constantly because the acronyms look similar and the pitch sounds identical.

articles.title: Amending Your Tax Return: When and How to File Form 1040-X
articles.covers: Discovering an error after you've already filed is more common than it feels in the moment, and it's rarely a crisis.

articles.title: Rental Property Income: How It's Actually Taxed
articles.covers: Rental income is taxable like any other income, but the deductions available against it are unusually generous compared to a typical job or side business — particularly one deduction that doesn't require spending any act

articles.title: Charitable Giving and Taxes: How Donations Actually Reduce Your Bill
articles.covers: Charitable giving is one of the most flexible parts of the tax code, and also one of the most misunderstood — largely because the tax benefit depends heavily on how you give, not just how much.

articles.title: Is Social Security Taxable? Understanding Tax on Retirement and Other Non-Wage Income
articles.covers: A lot of retirees assume Social Security benefits are simply tax-free.

articles.title: How to File Your Taxes for the First Time: A Complete Step-by-Step Guide
articles.covers: Filing for the first time? Here's the exact order of operations — what to gather, how to pick your filing status, and how to get a real number before you file, step by step.

articles.title: How to File Taxes as a Freelancer or 1099 Contractor: Step by Step
articles.covers: Freelance and 1099 income comes with a tax nobody withholds for you. Here's the exact step-by-step process — from tracking expenses to quarterly payments — plus where to run the real numbers.

articles.title: How to File Taxes With Both a W-2 Job and Freelance Income
articles.covers: Have a day job and side income? The two don't combine the way most people assume. Here's the step-by-step process for getting an accurate combined number instead of an unpleasant surprise.

articles.title: How to File State and Federal Taxes Together: A Step-by-Step Walkthrough
articles.covers: Federal and state taxes are calculated separately but need to be planned together. Here's the step-by-step process for getting a full combined picture, plus what changes if you moved or worked across state lines.

articles.title: How to File Taxes After a Major Life Change: New Job, Marriage, or Side Income
articles.covers: Marriage, a new job, or new side income all change your tax picture — but most people only find out how much at filing time. Here's how to check before then, step by step.

articles.title: How to File Taxes After Selling Stocks for the First Time
articles.covers: Sold stock, crypto, or a fund for the first time? Here's the exact step-by-step process for reporting it — cost basis, short vs. long-term, and where to run the real numbers.

articles.title: How to File Taxes in Retirement: Social Security, 401(k), and IRA Withdrawals
articles.covers: Filing with retirement income for the first time? Here's the step-by-step process for Social Security, 401(k)/IRA withdrawals, and RMDs — plus where to check the real numbers.

articles.title: How to File Taxes as an International Student or Visa Holder
articles.covers: Filing as an international student or visa holder starts with one question everything else depends on: resident or nonresident alien? Here's the step-by-step process to find out and file correctly.

articles.title: How to File Taxes If You Received Unemployment Income This Year
articles.covers: Lost a job and collected unemployment this year? Here's the step-by-step process for reporting it correctly — including the withholding trap most people don't know about.

articles.title: How to File Taxes When You Owe Money and Can't Pay in Full
articles.covers: Owe more than you can pay right now? Here's the step-by-step process — file anyway, understand the two different penalties, and set up a real payment plan instead of avoiding it.

articles.title: Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) 2026: Who Qualifies and How Much You Can Get
articles.covers: 2026 EITC maximum credit amounts by number of qualifying children, phase-in/phase-out mechanics, the $12,200 investment income cliff, childless-worker age rules (25-64), and state EITC piggyback credits.

articles.title: Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT): The 3.8% Surtax on Investment Income
articles.covers: NIIT mechanics: 3.8% rate, unindexed $200k/$250k/$125k MAGI thresholds, what counts as net investment income vs. what's excluded, the lesser-of calculation, and planning considerations.

articles.title: The Qualified Business Income (QBI) Deduction: The 20% Write-Off Explained
articles.covers: Section 199A QBI deduction for 2026: 20% deduction, $201,750/$403,500 thresholds and expanded $75k/$150k OBBBA phase-in range, SSTB vs non-SSTB treatment, W-2 wage/UBIA limitation, aggregation election, and the new $400 minimum deduction floor.

articles.title: Selling Your Home: The $250,000/$500,000 Capital Gains Exclusion Explained
articles.covers: Section 121 primary residence exclusion: $250k/$500k amounts, 2-of-5-year ownership/use test, once-per-2-years limit, partial exclusion for job/health/unforeseen-circumstances moves, depreciation recapture, inherited homes, and converted rentals/vacation homes.

articles.title: Quarterly Estimated Taxes: Safe Harbor Rules and How to Avoid the Underpayment Penalty
articles.covers: Estimated tax safe harbor: 90% current-year or 100%/110% prior-year (110% if prior AGI over $150k) tests, 2026 quarterly due dates, Form 2210 underpayment penalty, annualized income installment method for irregular income, and payment methods.

articles.title: Cryptocurrency Taxes: Form 1099-DA, Cost Basis, and What Actually Triggers a Taxable Event
articles.covers: Crypto tax mechanics: taxable vs non-taxable events, capital gains treatment, staking/mining/airdrops as ordinary income, Form 1099-DA phase-in (2025 gross proceeds only, 2026 basis reporting), account-by-account cost-basis method, and absence of a wash-sale rule for digital assets.

articles.title: 1099 Forms Explained: NEC, MISC, DIV, INT, and K — What Each One Means for Your Taxes
articles.covers: 1099-NEC/MISC/DIV/INT/K form types and what each reports; 2026 threshold changes: NEC/MISC $600→$2,000 (OBBBA), 1099-K reverted to $20,000/200 transactions; what to do with a missing or incorrect 1099.

articles.title: The Home Office Deduction: Simplified vs. Actual Expense Method
articles.covers: Home office deduction for self-employed: exclusive-and-regular-use test, simplified method ($5/sq ft, $1,500 max), actual expense method (Form 8829, depreciation and recapture), why W-2 employees generally cannot claim it post-TCJA.

articles.title: Divorce and Taxes: Filing Status, Alimony, and Claiming Kids After a Split
articles.covers: Tax implications of divorce: filing status by Dec 31 marital status, post-2018 alimony rules (not deductible/taxable, reversed from pre-2019 law), dependency/CTC assignment via Form 8332, Head of Household eligibility, splitting the home-sale exclusion, QDROs for retirement account division.

articles.title: The Kiddie Tax: How a Child's Unearned Income Gets Taxed at the Parent's Rate
articles.covers: Kiddie tax for 2026: $2,700 unearned income threshold ($1,350 two-tier floor), age/support tests, Form 8615 vs Form 8814 parental election, 529 plan exclusion, historical purpose (1986 income-shifting closure).

articles.title: Estate and Gift Tax Basics: The $15 Million Exemption and $19,000 Annual Exclusion
articles.covers: Federal estate/gift tax: $19,000 annual gift exclusion, $15,000,000 lifetime exemption (made permanent by OBBBA), gift-splitting, unlimited marital/charitable deduction, GST tax, 529 front-loading, and why almost no estates owe this tax.

articles.title: FBAR and FATCA: Reporting Foreign Bank Accounts and Assets
articles.covers: FBAR (FinCEN 114, $10,000 aggregate threshold) vs FATCA (Form 8938, filing-status/residency-dependent thresholds), penalties for non-willful/willful failure to file, Streamlined Filing Compliance Procedures, inherited foreign accounts.

articles.title: The Premium Tax Credit After the Enhanced Subsidies Expired: What Changed for 2026
articles.covers: ACA Premium Tax Credit after enhanced subsidies expired end of 2025: return of 400% FPL cliff, higher contribution percentages, Form 8962 reconciliation, repayment limitation caps, state supplemental subsidy programs.

articles.title: The 1031 Like-Kind Exchange: Deferring Capital Gains Tax on Real Estate
articles.covers: Section 1031 exchange: defers (not eliminates) gain, real-property-only since TCJA 2018, 45-day ID/180-day close deadlines, qualified intermediary requirement, boot/partial exchanges, depreciation recapture.

articles.title: IRS Penalties Explained: Failure to File, Failure to Pay, and Accuracy-Related Penalties
articles.covers: Failure-to-file (5%/month, 25% cap) vs failure-to-pay (0.5%/month, 25% cap) penalties, combined-penalty offset, interest, 20% accuracy-related penalty, reasonable-cause and first-time abatement.

articles.title: Innocent Spouse Relief: When You're Not Responsible for Your Spouse's Tax Debt
articles.covers: Joint-and-several liability on joint returns, three relief types (innocent spouse, separation of liability, equitable relief), 2-year deadline, Form 8857, factors IRS weighs.

articles.title: Gambling and Lottery Winnings: How They're Taxed (and the New 90% Loss Limit)
articles.covers: All gambling winnings taxable regardless of W-2G, OBBBA's new 90% cap on deductible losses starting 2026 (phantom income), raised $2,000 W-2G threshold for slots/bingo/keno, professional vs casual treatment, session recordkeeping.

articles.title: Tax-Loss Harvesting: Turning Investment Losses Into a Tax Break
articles.covers: Offsetting gains with losses (short/long-term matching), $3,000 annual limit against ordinary income with indefinite carryforward, 30-day wash-sale rule, crypto's current exemption from wash-sale, automated harvesting.

articles.title: The Nanny Tax: Household Employment Taxes Explained
articles.covers: $3,000 2026 FICA threshold for household employees (up from $2,800), separate $1,000/quarter FUTA threshold, exemptions (spouse/parent/child under 21/under 18), Schedule H, W-2 requirements, Dependent Care FSA interaction.

articles.title: Employee Stock Purchase Plans (ESPP): How the Discount and Sale Are Taxed
articles.covers: Qualified §423 ESPP: purchase discount/lookback provision, qualifying disposition (2yr from grant + 1yr from purchase) vs disqualifying disposition, ordinary income vs capital gain split, Form 3922/Schedule D, 1099-B basis errors.
